Your Amazon.com Inquiry
Thank you for writing to Amazon.com to bring this to our attention. The message you received was not sent to you by Amazon.com. We are investigating the situation and working to get that webpage removed. You did the right thing by contacting us. For your protection, we suggest that you never respond to requests for personal information that may be contained in the forged e- mail. I've provided some tips below my signature to help you identify spoof messages. I want to assure you that Amazon.com is not in the business of selling customer information. Many spammers and spoofers use programs that randomly generate e-mail addresses, in the hope that some percentage of these randomly-generated addresses will actually exist. Important Note: If you submitted your credit card number to the faked "Amazon.com" web site, we advise that you take steps to protect your information by notifying your credit card company. Additionally, you should delete that credit card from your Amazon.com account to prevent anyone from improperly regaining access to your account. To do so, click the link in your Seller Account to "Change your Amazon.com store-wide settings such as e- mail, address book, and 1-Click." Then click "Edit or delete a credit card" under Payment Settings in Your Account. Amazon.com has recently taken steps to combat e-mail forgeries such as this in the U.S. and Canada; you can read more about our efforts here: hxxp://www.amazon.com/stopspoofing I have included more information below my signature to assist you in identifying e-mail forgeries. If you encounter any other spoof messages in the future, please forward the messages to stop- spoofing@amazon.com. Thank you again for taking the time to contact us. Best regards, Amie M. Amazon.com Customer Service hxxp://www.amazon.com ============================== Check your order and more: hxxp://www.amazon.com/your-account 1. Amazon.com will *not* ask you for the following information in an e-mail message: * Your Amazon.com password * Your bank account information * Your credit card information or PIN * Your social security number * Your mother's maiden name or other information to identify you 2. Be on the lookout for poor grammar or typographical errors. Many phishing e-mails are translated from other languages or are sent without being proof-read. As a result, these messages can contain bad grammar or typographical errors. 3. Check the return address-- genuine e-mails come from an e-mail address ending in "@amazon.com". 4. Check the Web site address-- genuine Amazon.com Web sites always end with ".amazon.com" -- that is, "www[.]amazon.com." We *never* use a combination such as "security-amazon.com" or "amazon.com.biz". 5. When in doubt, go directly to the Amazon.com web site. Some phishing e-mails include a link that looks as though it will take you to your account, but it is really a shortened link to a completely different Web site. If you hover over the link in your e-mail client, you often can see the underlying, false Web address, either as a pop-up or as information in the browser status bar 6. Do not "unsubscribe"-- many spammers use these "unsubscribe" processes to create a list of valid, working e-mail addresses. Best regards, Amie M. **Quick Tips to Keep Your Account Safe:**

• Amazon will never ask for your password, credit card number, or social security number via email
• Always verify the sender's email address ends in @amazon.com
• Check that website URLs begin with www[.]amazon.com – we never use variations like amazon.com.biz
• When in doubt, log in directly to Amazon.com rather than clicking email links
• Be cautious of emails with spelling or grammar errors
• Never click "unsubscribe" on suspicious messages

For additional security information, visit: hxxp://www.amazon.com/stopspoofing

If you receive suspicious emails claiming to be from Amazon, forward them to stop-spoofing@amazon.com
